how_comparable_studies_use_it: Parent-child rank association is widely used for relative mobility comparisons across groups and cohorts.
supporting_studies: van der Weide et al. 2024; Torche 2015; Strömberg & Engzell 2025
why_fit_this_paper: Works well with repeated cross-sections and cross-wave harmonization when exact schooling scales differ across waves.
main_caveat: Sensitive to coding, ranking rules, and discrete education categories; should not be the only measure.

how_comparable_studies_use_it: Shows movement across education categories and highlights persistence at specific parental origins.
supporting_studies: Hertz et al. 2008; Causa & Johansson 2010; Brück & Esenaliev 2018
why_fit_this_paper: Makes the Uzbekistan results legible for policy audiences and compatible with locked education categories.
main_caveat: Can become sparse in small samples or when parent-child category pairs are missing.

how_comparable_studies_use_it: Often defined as child attainment exceeding parental attainment or leaving low-education origins.
why_fit_this_paper: Simple substantive interpretation for Uzbekistan and useful alongside persistence measures.
main_caveat: Affected by ceiling effects when parents already have high education.

how_comparable_studies_use_it: Used less often than upward mobility but helpful for showing whether gains are fragile or uneven.
supporting_studies: Comparative mobility practice; consistent with transition-matrix approaches
why_fit_this_paper: Adds symmetry and prevents one-sided reading of mobility as only upward movement.
main_caveat: Less common in headline comparisons and can be noisy in smaller subgroup cells.

how_comparable_studies_use_it: Captures exact-status reproduction in category space.
supporting_studies: Transition-matrix literature; Hertz et al. 2008; Brück & Esenaliev 2018
why_fit_this_paper: Useful for a paper centered on persistence, especially with harmonized ordinal categories.
main_caveat: Depends strongly on category design and can overstate immobility when categories are broad.

how_comparable_studies_use_it: OLS or ordered models on harmonized schooling categories are standard for associational intergenerational analysis.
why_fit_this_paper: Matches the locked Module B and preserves transparent interpretation with fixed effects.
main_caveat: Not causal; coefficient size depends on score mapping.

how_comparable_studies_use_it: Pandemic studies often use household surveys to describe devices, support, and interruption patterns when causal identification is limited.
supporting_studies: UNICEF 2020; Muñoz-Najar et al. 2021; Daniela et al. 2021; Iqbal et al. 2025
why_fit_this_paper: Fits the 2022–23 LiTS child module without overstating identification.
main_caveat: Should be labeled suggestive and kept separate from the main mobility results.
